CHAPTER THREE  
 
CHALLENGES FACING KENYA’S TAX SYSTEM 
 
Significant progress has been made in transforming the country’s tax system. However, the system 
still faces challenges which have had a negative impact on revenue performance. These challenges 
include: 
 
3.1 General Challenges 
a) Existence of hard to tax sectors   
The economy is dominated by a large informal sector, which is difficult hard to tax. The sector is 
characterized by poor record keeping, cash-based transactions and limited information due to its 
unregulated nature. For instance, the actual contribution to tax revenue of the agricultural sector, 
which forms a major part of this category, is not commensurate to the sector’s contribution to GDP 
which averages 21.4 percent. Previous efforts to tax this sector including the use of turnover tax 
and presumptive income tax have not realised the expected tax revenues. 
b) Tax incentives 
The tax laws provide various tax incentives in the form of tax exemptions, tax relief, allowances, 
tax deferral and concessional tax rates or timing rules such as accelerated depreciation of capital 
investments. Although, the incentives are aimed at promoting investments and providing relief to 
low-income earners and vulnerable groups in the society , they erode the tax base and cause the 
Government to forego tax revenue. This therefore has a negative impact on revenue mobilisation 
and implementation of the government programmes.  
c) Low tax compliance  
Tax compliance in terms of filing tax returns and payment of tax  due, stood at 70 percent in the 
FY 2020/21 and 77 percent in the FY 2021/22. The levels of compliance are mainly hindered by 
the technical and complex nature of tax laws and procedures, taxpayer apathy, high compliance 
cost, inadequate sharing of taxpayer information among National and County Government 
agencies, lack of physical presence of KRA offices in some regions of Kenya and low tax literacy 
among citizens. These factors make it difficult for taxpayers to file returns and pay taxes due.  
d)  Complexity in taxing emerging digital economy  
The tax system is not fully equipped to deal with emerging technological business models. This 
has led to some business activities being left out of the tax net especially activities carried out 
through the internet on digital platform s. These business activities can be carried out in a tax 
jurisdiction without having any physical presence in that jurisdiction. 
 
19 
 
3.2 Challenges to specific tax head 
 
a) Income Tax 
The taxation of income earned or derived in Kenya is faced with challenges which include:  
i) lack of clarity on taxation of some income derived or earned in Kenya;  
ii)  lack of progressivity in taxation of the personal income. The tax bands are narrow 
and an individual reaches the top bracket at very low income;  
iii) lack of guidelines for granting preferential tax rates; 
iv) challenges in administering income tax exemption since other Acts of Parliament 
provide for tax exemption; 
v) avoidance of tax by non-resident companies through repatriation of profits  and also 
to avoid the high corporation rate for non-residence;  
vi) lack of clarity on tax point on taxation o f gains from sale of property  leading to 
noncompliance;  
vii) complexity in taxation of some sectors;  
viii) deduction of expenditures which are not used for the production of income thus 
eroding the tax base; 
ix) lack of principles to guide investment allowance leading to some deduction exceeding 
100%; and 
x) multiple personal reliefs which erode tax base. 
 
b) Value Added Tax 
The challenges facing VAT include: 
(i) high tax expenditure – VAT tax expenditure is relatively high compared to the overall 
VAT collection;  
(ii) multiple rates – In the year 2023, VAT Act provide for the following rates:  8 percent 
for petroleum products, 16 percent for other goods/services and 0 percent for exported 
goods. The lower rate on petroleum goods creates a perverse incentive for relatively 
higher consumption;  
(iii)violation of the destination principle of some goods and services in the Second 
Schedule;  
(iv) lack of guiding principles for identifying goods or services that will be, taxable, 
exempt or zero rated; 
(v) low tax compliance; and  
 
20 
 
(vi) regressivity in case where exemption provides higher VAT benefit to the richer 
section in comparison to the poor and vulnerable. 
c) Excise Duty  
The challenges facing excise duty regime in Kenya include: 
i) high excise duty rate for goods and services compared with the o ther EAC Partner 
States. This contributes to illicit trade across borders such as smuggling. High excise 
duty rates also discourage investment in the sector and distort the market;  
ii) narrow tax bases as only few goods and services are subject to excise duty;  
iii) lack of principles in identifying goods and services subject to excise duty; and. 
iv) low compliance due to existence of illicit trade and, counterfeit excisable goods.  This 
reduces revenues accruing from excise duty  to the Government and creates unfair 
burden on compliant manufacturers and importers of excisable goods.  
 
d)  Customs Administration 
Dynamics in international trade  including, emerging business models, new trading partners, 
increased security threats, organized crimes and increasing number of regional free trade areas that 
Kenya is a member  of, present challenges in customs administration . Specifically, customs 
administration faces the following main challenges; 
i) low levels of automation in some custom processes creating loopholes for revenue 
leakage. 
ii) inadequate; 
I. modern technological tools at entry/exit border points to detect and deter 
cross border smuggling, mis-declaration, undervaluation and 
misclassification of goods;  
II. cargo monitoring and management; 
III. staff capacity and training to handle the ever-changing business 
environment; 
IV. knowledge by importers and their agents on custom processes;  
V. cooperation, coordination and collaboration amongst Government agencies 
on customs and border management matters; and 
VI. Customs-to-Customs regional and international cooperation to curb cases 
of abuse of rules of origin, cargo diversion and undervaluation. 
iii) porous borders which create room for illicit trade. 
 
 
21 
 
3.3 International Taxation and Tax Treaties 
There is significant presence of M ultinational National Enterprises (MNEs) that derive income 
from Kenya and constitute a significant source of revenue for the Government, including Kenyan 
companies that are fast expanding into other African  and world markets. Most of the treaties that 
have been negotiated do not have provisions to address base erosion and profit shifting leading to 
exploitation of the se gaps by some businesses to avoid paying taxes lawfully due in Kenya. 
Specifically, Kenya is faced with the following tax treaties and international taxation challenges: 
a) lack of policy guidelines for negotiation of bilateral, multilateral, regional and international 
tax agreements with regards to avoidance of double taxation, Customs tarrifs and fiscal 
evasion; 
b) some DTAs in force were concluded many years ago hence not in line with the recent 
international best practice; 
c) abuse of tax treaties;  
d) inadequate mechanism to address base erosion and profit shifting by MNEs;  
e) insufficient technical capacity on international taxation; and 
f) emerging and evolving business models that cannot be adequately addressed by existing 
taxation frameworks. 
 
3.4 Tax Administration 
KRA collects and account for tax revenue on behalf of the National Government. In carrying out 
this mandate, the Authority faces the following challenges: 
a) high number of unregistered taxpayers; 
b) low taxpayer morale; 
c) limited access to taxpayer transactional information; 
d)  inadequate capacity due to skills gap in handling emerging challenges; and 
e) limited collaboration and information sharing between the National and County 
Governments on tax matters;  
3.5 Tax Dispute Resolution 
Tax dispute resolution process in Kenya, in most cases is adversarial occasioning a high number 
of disputes at the Tax Appeals Tribunal and in the Courts.  Such disputes lock revenue over long 
periods of time.   
 
22 
 
3.6 Unpredictable Tax Rates and Tax Bases  
The growth of government expenditure without corresponding growth of tax revenue has 
necessitated regular review of tax laws to expand the tax base. However, expansion of the tax base 
is limited by the  structure of the economy which is dominated by informal sector  and other 
structural factors. Frequent changes in tax rates and review of tax laws create unpredictability in 
the government policy. 
  
 
23 
 
CHAPTER FOUR  
 
POLICY GUIDELINES 
This chapter provides policy guidelines to address the challenges identified in chapter three as 
follows:  
4.1 Hard to tax sectors  
This policy aims at progressively increasing tax yields from agriculture and the informal sectors. 
To achieve this objective, the Government will: 
a) Explore ways of enhancing the taxation of property transfer s including land and 
agricultural sector; 
b) Explore ways of enhancing taxation of informal sectors including through increasing 
KRA’s presence in big town and cities; 
c)  Exploring mechanism for collecting taxes from the informal sector such as 
appointment of tax collection agents; 
d) Roll out education programmes to farmers and informal sector groups on taxation;  
e) Enhance collaborations and exchange of information on taxpayers between the 
National Government and County Governments. 
 
4.2 Management of Tax Expenditure   
This policy aims at enhancing the management of the tax expenditure framework and ensuring 
analysis of the benefits is done before any tax expenditure is approved for implementation while 
rationalizing the exi sting tax expenditures . In principle, tax incentives and exemptions are 
economically inefficient, inequitable, adds to the complexity of the tax system and reduce revenue 
yield. In order to address this situation, the Government will: 
a) Review the current tax expenditure and formulate a  framework for granting tax 
incentive; 
b) Develop and regularly review guidelines for granting tax incentives  taking into 
consideration the costs , benefits, promotion  of investment and cushioning Kenyans 
against economic shocks;   
c) Maintain a record of all tax expenditures; 
d) Comprehensively review tax incentives after every five years to align them with the 
Government’s agenda; 
e) Regularly request companies that enjoy tax incentives to provide data on the impact of 
the incentives on their business and contribution to the growth of the economy  
 
24 
 
f) Provide sunset clause for specific tax incentives granted to  specific sectors  where 
applicable;  
g) Develop and implement a centralized monitoring and evaluation framework for tax 
incentives;  
h) Prepare and publicise tax expenditure reports annually; and 
i) Provide highlights in the annual Budget Statement tax expenditure for the previous 
year. 
 
4.3 Increase in tax compliance level 
In order t o progressively increase tax compliance levels  and reduce tax disputes between the 
revenue administrator and the taxpayers, the Government will:  
a) Continuously review the mechanism s for detecting, deterring and sanctioning 
incidences of integrity in tax administration;  
b) Continuously leverage on the  use of modern information technology in tax 
administration services and maintain up to date taxpayer records;  
c) Integrate tax and customs administration systems with relevant government agencies 
and other third-party systems; 
d) Regularly provide clarification on any issues that may arise in the administration of tax 
laws; 
e) Regularly review tax administration procedures to address gaps and emerging issues; 
f) Enhance implementation and monitoring of a structured and tailor -made engagement 
program for stakeholders; 
g) Strengthen the mechanism of educating different segment of taxpayers on changes to 
the tax laws and procedures; 
h) Enhance taxation awareness in the Kenyan education system; 
i) Penalize non-compliance and recognize compliant taxpayers; 
j) Enhance the visibility and presence of the tax administration across the country 
including through enhanced surveillance and establishment of satellite offices; 
k) Enhance risk-based verification programs for detecting and deterring non-compliance;  
l) Enhance framework for a tax audit process, in consultation with stakeholders, to ensure 
transparency, certainty and predictability of the audit process; and 
m) Enhance public awareness on revenue collection and utilization for improved service 
delivery. 
 
25 
 
4.4 Taxation of Emerging Digital Economy 
To progressively increase tax yields from emerging digital economy, the Government will: 
a) Leverage on technology to detect tax avoidance and enhance compliance of emerging 
business transactions on digital or electronic platforms; 
b) Regularly review the tax regime that apply to digital marketplace to ensure optimal tax 
collection while ensuring compliance with the international consensus o n taxation of 
the digital economy; 
c) Invest in continuous training of tax administrators  and other key stakeholders  on 
emerging technologies; 
d) Continuously review tax laws to align them with emerging technologies; and 
e) Collaborate with relevant stakeholders to enhance taxation of the digital economy. 
 
4.5 Personal Income Tax 
Personal Income tax shall be guided by the following guidelines:   
a) All income derived from or accrued in Kenya shall be subject to tax unless exempted under 
international agreements to which Kenya is party to; 
b) The aggregate income shall be taxed in accordance with a progressive rate structure; 
c) The PAYE tax bands will be reviewed and designed to provide for progressive taxation, 
and a tax-free threshold or personal relief; 
d) Savings for retirement benefit will be subject to exempt-exempt-exempt (EEE) method of 
taxation where (i) contributions to retirement benefits scheme shall be  deductible subject 
to the set threshold, (ii) investment income of the retirement benefits scheme shall be  
exempt from taxation, and (iii) pension payments from the retirement benefits scheme shall 
be exempt from taxation.   
e) Review all income tax reliefs such as personal, mortgage, and insurance reliefs every five 
years;  
f) Review all thresholds and the tax bands every five years for inflation adjustment; and 
g) Periodic studies will be undertaken to inform review of the progressivity of the tax band 
and optimum level of taxation to avoid a Laffer Curve effect. 
 
4.6 Corporate Income Tax 
Corporate Income Tax shall be guided by the following: 
a) The tax base for corporate income tax shall include all revenues sourced in Kenya on an 
accrual basis regardless of whether derived from business or any other activity; 
 
26 
 
b) In the computation of taxable income from a business, deduction shall be allowed in respect 
to any expenditure incurred wholly and exclusively for the purposes of generating th at 
income;  
c) The capital deductions allowable shall not exceed 100 percent of the actual cost of the 
investment or the asset; 
d) There shall be a single corporate tax rate ; No preferential tax rate shall be granted. 
However, where a preferential rate is granted for specific government policy objectives, 
the tax rate shall not be lower than 50% of the general rate of corporation tax;  
e) Exemption, if any, from income tax shall be provided in the Income Tax Act only; 
f) Repatriated profits for non-residents operating in Kenya through permanent establishment 
shall be subjected to tax at a rate equivalent to that charged to dividends paid to non -
residents; 
g) There shall be a simplified method of taxation of income in cases of inherent difficulty in 
collection and tax administration on some sectors;  
h) There shall be a withholding tax on payment for some services and some income such as 
dividends, royalties, and interest payment;  
i) The investment allowance shall be spread over the period specified under the Income Tax 
Act;  
j) The method of calculating the investment allowance shall be the straight-line method; and. 
k) Periodic studies will be undertaken to inform review of the tax rates, tax incentives and 
optimum level of taxation to avoid a Laffer Curve effect as well as taxation of income from 
Gaming, Betting and Lotteries. 
 
4.7 Capital Gains Tax 
Studies will be undertaken to inform taxation of capital gains from transfer of property with 
international best practice including the tax rate to be charged on the gain and also how to 
address the inflation effect in the taxation of the gains. 
 
4.8 Value Added Tax 
VAT shall be guided by the following guidelines:   
a) The tax base for VAT shall be the domestic consumption of all goods and taxable services;  
b) Exemption from VAT will be based on consideration of compliance and administration 
costs or need to provide incentives to investors or cushioning the Kenyans from economic 
shocks;  
 
27 
 
c) VAT shall be charged at all stages of production and distribution, including  at the retail 
stage;  
d) There shall be a general VAT rate; 
e) Despite paragraph (d) above, the government may provide for a lower VAT rate than the 
general rate to cushion the economy against shocks occasioned by global trends leading to 
adverse effects of price increase of these products; 
f) No exemption or lower -than-standard rate of VAT shall be provided to any person on 
distributional consideration; 
g) All goods or taxable services for export shall be  taxed at a rate of  zero to promote 
competitiveness in the international market and in accordance with destination principle1;  
h) All goods or taxable services shall be charged VAT whether they are imported or produced 
locally;  
i) There shall be a threshold of the value of taxable supplies beyond which a person will be 
required to be registered and charge VAT;  
j) The VAT threshold shall be reviewed from time to time to align it with the prevailing 
economic situation;   
k) Voluntary registration for VAT may be allowed for a person who has not met the prescribed 
threshold;  
l) Supplies of vatable goods or services may be required to be registered for VAT irrespective 
of the threshold; 
m) The Government may put in place information technology system to facilitate enforcement 
of compliance with the VAT Act; and. 
n) Periodic studies will be undertaken to inform review of the tax rate, tax incentives and 
optimum level of taxation to avoid a Laffer Curve effect. 
 
4.9 Excise Duty 
Excise Duty shall be guided by the following guidelines:   
a) The imposition of excise duty shall be based on the destination principle;  
b) Excise duty shall be charged on goods and services with negative externalities to 
discourage consumption due to their harmful nature and negative impact on health of the 
 
 
1 Destination principle in regard to VAT means charging VAT on goods or taxable services at the jurisdiction of   
final consumption of the goods or the taxable services. 
 
28 
 
consumer, environment, and the social evils that are caused by excess consumption and 
use of such goods and services;  
c) Despite the general principal of charging excise duty, specified under paragraph 4.9(b) 
above, excise duty may be charged on any other goods or services primarily for raising 
revenue;  
d) The specific goods and services subject to excise duty shall be as provided for in the Excise 
Duty Act; 
e) The rate of excise duty shall be either ad valorem, specific or both; 
f) In determining whether to impose specific or ad valorem rate of excise duty, consideration 
shall be made to ensure no undue advantage is conferred to any category of goods; 
g) Excise duty paid on raw materials used in the manufacture of excisable products shall be 
offset against the excise duty payable on the finished goods; 
h) Excise duty shall not apply to the goods or services supplied to persons with privileges and 
immunities in accordance with international agreements and conventions, where Kenya is 
a party;   
i) All exemptions or variation of excise duty rates on goods or service s hall be provided in 
the Excise Duty Act; 
j) Manufacturing, or supply of excisable goods and services shall be undertaken by a 
licensed/registered person; 
k) In the review of the excise duty rates or the structure of the excise duty regime the 
Government shall engage stakeholders;  
l)  The Government may put in place information technology system to facilitate enforcement 
of compliance with the Excise Duty Act;   
m) Periodic studies will be undertaken to inform review of the excise duty rate, tax incentives 
and optimum level of taxation to avoid a Laffer Curve effect;  
n) The Government may dedicate part of excise duty revenue for use in the mitigation of the 
negative effects to the society emanating from consumption of goods or services subject to 
excise duty; and. 
o) Studies will regularly be undertaken on tax structure of excisable goods and services  
including excise duty on Gaming, Betting and Lotteries  to determine the optimal rate of 
tax. 
 
 
29 
 
4.10 Fees and Levies 
Government will regularly review fees and levies imposed by National Government. To implement 
this the Government will develop a framework for imposition of fees and levies . The framework 
may take into account the need to protection local industries, provide for certainty and 
predictability of the business environment as well as raising revenue.  
 
4.11 Customs Administration 
To enhance customs administration, the Government will: 
a) Ensure u se of modern technology and enhance automation of customs procedures and 
processes; 
b) Continuously review the adequacy of the border posts/entry points for effective border 
management; 
c) Continuously build capacity of staff on customs matters; 
d) Enhance stakeholder education and awareness on custom processes; 
e) Enhance cooperation between custom administration and MDAs; 
f) Cooperate with neighboring countries for establishment of more one-stop border posts; 
g) Enhance customs-to-customs cooperation to ensure exchange of information. 
h) Enhance monitoring of transit  cargo, transfers and exports  to curb transit or transfer of 
cargo diversions; 
i) Adopt strategies to continually address emerging trade patterns/trends such as cargo 
consolidation and trade agreements; and 
j) Enhance measures to detect and deter illicit trade. 
k) Subject any amendment to the East African Community Customs Management Act and 
East African Community Common External Tariffs to stakeholders’ consultation and 
approval by the National Assembly.  
 
4.12 Tax Administration 
The following policy guidelines shall apply to Tax Administration:  
a) The tax administration shall enhance access to mechanisms of registration and the 
registration of all potential taxpayers; 
b) The tax administration shall leverage on technology to facilitate registration and 
categorization of registered persons as per obligation and segmentation of sectors for ease 
of administration; 
 
30 
 
c) There shall be continuous capacity development in revenue administration through training 
of staff, acquisition of equipment and technology to deal with emerging tax issues;  
d) Review guidelines on tax offset or refund processes with a view to making the process 
effective and efficient; 
e) Enhance automation of tax refund process through integration of tax and customs 
administration systems; 
f) Progressively adopt a unified system for collection of taxes, fees and levies for the National 
and County Governments; 
g) Enhance collaboration in revenue mobilization and information sharing between the 
National Government, County Governments and MDAs;  
h) Continuously enhance the system to facilitate registration or deregistration of taxpayers ; 
and 
i) Automate process of tax exemption to enhance efficiency, transparency and accountability 
in exemption processing.   
 
4.13 Tax Dispute Resolution  
To address the challenges with tax dispute resolution, the Government will: 
a) Review the tax dispute resolution process es to reduce the cost and time for disputes 
resolution;  
b) Establish a technical committee to deal with public and private rulings on a regular basis;  
c) Provide for autonomy of the out of court or out of Tax Appeals Tribunal process of tax 
dispute resolution;  
d) Establish a high court that will specialize on tax matters; and  
e) Enhance capacity for the Judiciary to deal with tax matters. 
 
4.14 International Taxation and Tax Treaties  
To progressively address tax malpractices, provide clarity, predictability and coherence  to 
international tax laws relating to cross border transactions, the Government will; 
a) Develop rules in line with international best practice to address base erosion and profit 
shifting risks; 
b) Enhance capacity of tax administration in dealing with cross border transactions and 
transfer pricing;  
 
31 
 
c) Coordinate with other tax jurisdictions to address issues on taxation of cross border 
transactions; 
d) Negotiate bilateral, multilateral, regional and international tax agreements with regards 
to avoidance of double taxation, C ustoms duty and fiscal evasion guided by the 
following principles: 
i) Analysis of the benefits vis-a-vis costs to achieve beneficial outcome to 
Kenya; 
ii)  Existence of significant trade between the two countries; 
iii) The prevention of tax evasion and discrimination;  
iv) Certainty and simplicity in addressing cross border activities; 
v) Partnerships in conducting joint tax audits with other jurisdictions where 
such agreements are in place; 
vi) The impact of domestic legislation in the implementation of treaties 
(Limitation of Benefit Clauses); 
vii)  Opportunities for mutual and technical assistance in capacity building; 
viii) Exchange of information and the collection of taxes; 
e) Periodically review DTAs to align with international best practice;  
f) Engage in bilateral and multilateral initiatives in building an international consensus on 
new and innovative international taxation tools;  
g) Develop a legal framework and infrastructure to facilitate automatic exchange of 
information and country-by-country reporting;  
h) Domesticate international best practice arrangements to deal with base erosion and 
profit shifting; and  
i) Upon completion of the negotiation DTAs shall be submitted to the National Assembly 
for ratification in accordance with the Treaty Making and Ratification Act, 2012 as well as the 
Statutory Instruments Act, 2013. 
4.15 Predictability in Tax Rates and Tax Bases  
The Policy aims at providing a framework to guide, to a reasonable degree, predictable and 
sustainable rates of tax as well as widening of the tax base. To achieve these objectives , the 
Government will undertake: 
a) Comprehensive review of tax laws every five years to align with other Government policies 
and international best practice; 
b) Stakeholder engagement during review of tax laws and assess ment of the impact of the 
proposed changes on tax revenue and investment;  
 
32 
 
c) Periodic review of the tax laws to keep abreast with emerging trends in the business 
environment and the Government’s agenda; and  
d) A review to address emergencies, pandemics or natural disasters at any time such cases 
occur. The existing tax legislation and administration will be reformed to align with this 
Policy in a phased manner.  
For this purpose, Government alongside this Policy developed a four-year Medium Term Revenue 
Strategy (MTRS) covering the period 2024/25 - 2026/27 that contains specific reform measures 
necessary to move towards a modern tax system which will significantly enhance revenue 
mobilization.  The MTRS will improve the predictability of tax rates thus facilitating investment 
decisions.

CHAPTER SIX 
 
RISK MANAGEMENT, MONITORING AND EVALUATION  
6.1 Introduction 
This Chapter outlines the approaches that will be used for risk management, monitoring and 
evaluation in the implementation of the National Tax Policy.  
 
6.2 Monitoring and Evaluation 
The Policy will be implemented through strategies that will set out specific measures and the 
timeline for the implementation. Among the strategies   to implement this Policy is the Medium -
Term Revenue Strategies (MTRS). The implementation  will be tracked using  a set of key 
indicators outlined in the various policy implementation documents . The National Treasury will  
oversee and evaluate the Policy’s implementation, with a scheduled review  every 5 years or any 
other period as may be necessary, in consultation with stakeholders 
 
6.3 Risk Management 
The implementation of this Policy is pegged on proper risk identification, analysis and mitigation. 
Risk identification and analysis enabled development of possible risk mitigation measures meant 
to reduce the consequences or the likelihood of their occurrence.  
The National Treasury take the lead in the implementation of the risk  mitigation measures 
identified in the Policy. KRA, being the main implementer of the policy measures, will provide 
regular feedback on the challenges faced in the implementation.  Other stakeholders will be 
expected to provide feedback on the risks associat ed with the tax policy measures implemented  
over time.  
 
6.4 The Risk Register 
The risks that are likely to affect the implementation of the Policy have been identified and their 
likelihood, consequence and their severity analysed. Mitigation actions for each risk have been 
identified and together are presented in the Risk Register presented below. 
The risks identified during implementation of the Policy will be assessed and proposed mitigation 
measures recorded in the register to facilitate monitoring of the status of the implementation. The 
risk register will be updated regularly during the implementation of the policy  and shall contain 
the type of t he risk, the level  of the risk and the proposed mitigation measures as shown in the 
table below. The risk level will be classified as either high (H), medium (M) or low (L) depending 
on the likelihood of occurrence and severity.

Article 227 of the Constitution of Kenya requires that when a state organ or any other 
public entity contracts for goods or services, it shall do so in accordance with a system 
that is fair equitable transparent, competitive and cost effective.
Section 7(1) of the Public Procurement and Asset Disposal Act 2015, (The Act) 
states that the National Treasury established under section 11 of the Public 
Finance Management Act 2012 shall be responsible for Public Procurement and 
Asset Disposal Policy formulation.  In exercise of this mandate, Section 7(2)© 
of the Act requires the National Treasury to design and prescribe an efficient 
procurement management system for the national and county governments to 
ensure transparent procurement and asset disposal as contemplated by Article 
227 of the Constitution: Provided that the National Treasury shall prescribe 
through Regulations a system under this paragraph which operates, respects and 
promotes the distinctiveness of the national and county levels of government;
The Public Procurement and Asset Disposal Act 2015 came into effect in January 
2016. and is now ripe for review to be in line with emerging issues, international 
best practices and address gaps and lessons learnt so far.
The said legal framework which has been in operation for period of 9 years, 
has been critical in streamlining the Public Procurement system in Kenya, inter 
alia, providing for efficient public procurement; providing clarity on the public 
procurement procedures and methods; emphasizing the role of professionals in 
public procurement; streamlining the roles and responsibilities of various actors 
in public procurement ; providing for eProcurement; and laying emphasis on 
robust contract management and Asset Management.
In line with Articles 232(1)(d) of the Constitution, the National Treasury hereby 
invites procurement practitioners, members of the public, national and  county 
governments, non-governmental organizations and civil societies, private 
sector players and stakeholders to submit comments, views and memoranda 
on the proposed areas for review of the Act with a view to enhance fairness, 
transparency, competitiveness, cost- effectiveness and eradicate avenues of 
corruption, conflict of interest and abuse of office in the public sector public 
procurement as required in Article 227 of the Constitution, all geared towards 
obtaining value for money.
Persons interested in submitting comments are requested to scan the QR code 
that can also be accessed in the National Treasury website www.treasury.go.ke, 
PPRA website https://ppra.go.ke and KISM website htps://kism.or.ke and complete 
the feeback form therein.
   
 
Alternatively, persons may submit written memoranda in hard copies, in the 
format provided for in the National Treasury website, addressed to the Principal 
Secretary, National Treasury, P .O. Box 30007 Nairobi-00100 , or email to 
ppd@treasury.go.ke with a copy to dppd4553@gmail.com to be received by 11th 
October, 2024.
